reasoning:
The Federal Reserve has officially entered a rate-cutting cycle as of late 2025, having cut rates by 25 basis points in September 2025 and forecasting at least two additional cuts before the end of the year. The 12-month certificate of deposit (CD) national rate (NDR12MCD) is highly correlated with Fed rate expectations and has been steadily declining from 1.84% in Nov 2024 to 1.64% by Nov 2025. Given the macroeconomic environment of cooling inflation and an intentional Federal Reserve policy easing, CD rates are very likely to continue dropping or remain flat. Historical changes in this metric show a steady month-over-month decrease of 1 to 4 basis points during expected easing, except for occasional short-lived anomalies. 

By December 2025, the impact of further late-2025 Fed cuts will be priced in, making an increase in the CD rate highly unlikely. By February 2026, the downward trend should become even more pronounced as the target federal funds rate drops closer to 3.5%, leaving very little runway for retail deposit rates to jump above their November 2025 benchmark. Uncertainty increases slightly for the longer February horizon due to potential pauses in the Fed's rate-cutting cycle or minor upward data anomalies, but the probability of a net increase relative to November 2025 remains very low.
